If she had anticipated the question, she would have stayed calm.
If we had shown the risk, they would not have pushed back.
If I had conceded the point, the meeting would have moved on.
They would have invested if the plan had addressed the red flag.
Had we prepared for the follow-up, we could have closed the round.
So, the pitch went well. Then the questions started. How do you think you handled the grilling?
Honestly? If I had anticipated the hardest question, I would have stayed calm. I hesitated.
Which question caught you?
The one about the red flag in year one. I hedged instead of naming the worst-case scenario.
That is the lesson. If you had shown the contingency first, they would not have pushed back so hard.
I know. Next time I will concede the fair point quickly and reassure the room.
Exactly. And do not deflect a sharp objection. A good objection tells you where to look.
So the follow-up questions were not attacks. They were curiosity.
Now you understand. If you welcome the pushback, the room trusts you. That is what closes a round.
If I had anticipated that question, I would have stayed calm.
That is a fair objection -- let me concede the point.
Let me name the worst-case scenario before you do.
I welcome the pushback; it shows me the red flag.
If we had shown the contingency, they would not have pushed back.

Hi Eliane, it is your pitch coach. One thought before your next Q and A. The room is not testing your slides, it is testing you under pressure. When they grill you, do not deflect and do not hedge. If you had watched last week's meeting, you would have seen it: the founders who win are the ones who concede a fair point and then reassure the room. Anticipate the hardest question, and name the worst-case scenario before the investor finds the red flag. Remember, a sharp objection is almost a gift. If you had welcomed the pushback last time instead of fearing it, every follow-up question would have become a chance to prove your plan. Field the hard ones well, and the easy ones take care of themselves.

Hi team, it is Eliane. Let me run a quick post-mortem on the Q and A. Two things I would have changed. First, if I had anticipated the question about year one, I would have named the worst-case scenario myself, instead of hedging. Second, if we had shown the contingency in the deck, they would not have pushed back so hard on the numbers. Here is what we do next time. We do not deflect a single objection; we treat each one as a red flag we get to fix in the room. We concede the fair points fast, we reassure with data, and we welcome every follow-up question. If we grill ourselves before the investors do, the real Q and A will feel like a conversation, not a test.
